proposals.hypothesis: The empirical mWUE-aridity relationship, calibrated across modern ecosystems using flux tower data, can be inverted to reconstruct past aridity indices (e.g., vapor pressure deficit) from fossil plant cellulose δ13C and δ18O records, providing more constrained estimates of paleo-hydroclimate than isotopic data alone.
proposals.method: We will first establish a robust modern baseline by calculating mWUE (net ecosystem exchange / evapotranspiration) from FLUXNET2015 and AmeriFlux data for key western US ecosystems (forests, grasslands, wetlands). This mWUE will be regressed against site aridity (VPD, AI). Concurrently, we will compile bulk leaf and cellulose δ13C and δ18O measurements from these sites. Using a Bayesian hierarchical model, we will integrate these datasets to create a transfer function linking isotopic signatures to mWUE and aridity. This function will then be applied to high-resolution δ13C and δ18O time-series from Late Holocene and Pleistocene packrat midden macrofossils and speleothems.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, process-based framework for quantitative paleo-aridity reconstruction, directly bridging modern ecosystem physiology and paleoclimatology. It will yield spatially explicit reconstructions of past water stress gradients in semi-arid regions like California, testing climate model simulations of hydroclimate variability over key transitions.
proposals.novelty: While paleo-isotope studies are common, the explicit use of the modern mWUE concept—a direct product of ecosystem flux research—as a calibration tool for interpreting these records is a novel, mechanistic advance beyond simple empirical correlations between isotopes and climate.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Magnitude of Net Ecosystem Exchange Bias: A Multi-Method Partitioning Analysis to Constrain Respiration and Photosynthesis in a Semi-Arid Ecosystem
proposals.motivation: A critical synthesis by Wehr et al. (2016) established that standard flux partitioning methods systematically underestimate ecosystem respiration (Re) and gross primary production (GPP) by ~30%, directly impacting carbon cycle quantification. This bias is derived from comparisons with chamber and isotopic methods, but these reference methods themselves have significant limitations and uncertainties, particularly in complex, water-limited ecosystems. The true magnitude of the underestimation in net ecosystem exchange (NEE) remains poorly constrained, risking misinterpretation of ecosystem carbon sink strength and its response to climate stressors like drought.
proposals.hypothesis: The reported 30% systematic underestimation of Re and GPP from eddy-covariance partitioning is itself an underestimate. This larger bias arises from fundamental, shared limitations in both traditional (e.g., nighttime flux, light-response) and reference (e.g., chamber, isotopic) partitioning methods during periods of high ecosystem stress, such as soil moisture limitation and high temperatures.
proposals.method: We will conduct an intensive campaign at a well-instrumented, water-stressed Californian grassland or savanna site (leveraging Baldocchi's existing flux networks). We will deploy a novel multi-method partitioning framework, running in parallel: 1) standard eddy-covariance algorithms (e.g., Reichstein's nighttime method, Lasslop's daytime method), 2) continuous chamber-based soil and stem respiration measurements, and 3) tunable diode laser absorption spectroscopy for Keeling plot isotopic (δ13C) partitioning of net fluxes. A Bayesian state-space model will be developed to assimilate all concurrent data streams (CO2, H2O, CH4, temperature, soil moisture, isotopic signatures), explicitly quantifying the covariance of errors between methods to isolate the most probable true values of Re and GPP.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, empirically constrained estimate of the true systematic bias in flux-partitioned carbon fluxes, moving beyond a single correction factor. It will yield a transparent Bayesian framework for quantifying partitioning uncertainty. The results will directly inform best practices for interpreting long-term flux data from semi-arid ecosystems, which are critical yet vulnerable components of the global carbon cycle.
proposals.novelty: While the 30% bias is known, questioning its own accuracy by confronting the limitations of the reference methods themselves is a novel, higher-order critique. The integration of continuous methane and isotopic data with traditional partitioning within a formal Bayesian error-propagation framework represents a significant methodological advance over prior comparative studies.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Carbon Sink Paradigm: Quantifying the Net Ecosystem Carbon Balance of Moderate- vs. High-Productivity Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: The prevailing paradigm for maximizing ecosystem carbon sequestration focuses on maximizing gross primary productivity (GPP). However, high GPP is often coupled with high ecosystem respiration (Reco), potentially diminishing net carbon uptake. This is critical for managing ecosystems as carbon sinks under climate change, especially in water-limited regions like California where high productivity can exacerbate water stress and respiration losses.
proposals.hypothesis: Ecosystems with moderate GPP but substantially minimized Reco will achieve a superior, more stable net ecosystem carbon balance (NECB) than ecosystems with high GPP but proportionally high Reco, when integrated over seasonal and multi-year timescales.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site synthesis and targeted field campaign using the eddy covariance technique to measure CO2, H2O, and CH4 fluxes. Sites will be selected from existing networks (e.g., AmeriFlux, ICOS) and new installations to pair ecosystems of contrasting productivity (e.g., perennial grasslands vs. irrigated alfalfa, mature vs. recently disturbed wetlands). We will partition net ecosystem exchange (NEE) into GPP and Reco using standard nighttime-based and daytime partitioning models. The analysis will focus on the GPP:Reco ratio and its sensitivity to environmental drivers like soil temperature and water table depth, integrating chamber-based soil respiration and methane flux measurements where applicable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating ecosystem carbon sequestration efficiency beyond GPP alone. It will directly inform land management and restoration strategies by identifying the metabolic and environmental conditions that optimize NECB, particularly in the context of water-carbon trade-offs highlighted in semi-arid ecosystems and managed wetlands.
proposals.novelty: This explicitly tests the non-intuitive idea that the most productive ecosystem is not always the strongest carbon sink, shifting focus from gross fluxes to the net balance controlled by respiration. It integrates Dr. Baldocchi's expertise in water-carbon fluxes and methane to provide a holistic view of greenhouse gas balance.

proposals.title: Resolving the Carbon Sink Paradox in Old-Growth Forests: The Role of Water Availability, Disturbance Legacy, and Methane Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 consensus often holds that old-growth forests are carbon-neutral, yet long-term flux tower data from sites like Wind River and Howland suggest many remain persistent carbon sinks. This paradox challenges fundamental models of ecosystem carbon dynamics and undermines accurate projections of forest carbon sequestration under climate change. Resolving the mechanisms behind this sustained sink is critical for informing conservation and climate policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the persistent carbon sink in many old-growth forests is maintained by a combination of (1) enhanced water-use efficiency and deep-water access buffering productivity against drought, and (2) a legacy of past, low-intensity disturbances creating structural heterogeneity that drives continuous, patch-level growth, offsetting ecosystem respiration.
proposals.method: We will leverage and synthesize long-term (20+ year) eddy covariance data from established old-growth forest sites in AmeriFlux and FLUXNET networks. The analysis will employ a process-partitioning framework, using algorithms like REddyProc for gap-filling and flux partitioning to separate gross primary productivity (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (Reco). We will test mechanisms by correlating interannual variability in net ecosystem exchange (NEE) with key drivers: soil moisture profiles, vapor pressure deficit, and indices of structural heterogeneity derived from airborne lidar. Methane fluxes will be concurrently analyzed to assess if methane oxidation acts as a non-CO2 carbon sink, as suggested by Baldocchi's wetland work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, data-driven explanation for the old-growth carbon sink paradox, moving beyond the 'neutral' assumption. It will quantify the relative importance of hydraulic and disturbance-legacy mechanisms. The findings will directly improve the parameterization of ecosystem and earth system models (e.g., CLM, ORCHIDEE) for mature forest stands.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating water flux and methane dynamics with carbon cycling to explain old-growth sink strength, and in applying a disturbance-legacy framework from ecosystem ecology to interpret long-term flux records, moving from a 'steady-state' to a 'dynamic mosaic' model of forest carbon balance.

proposals.title: Decoupling of Ecosystem Photosynthesis and Respiration During Compound Drought-Heat Events: A Multi-Site Flux Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem models often assume gross primary production (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (Reco) respond proportionally to drought, simplifying carbon cycle feedbacks. However, evidence from semi-arid ecosystems suggests these fluxes can decouple, with Reco sometimes remaining elevated relative to GPP during heat spells, turning ecosystems into transient carbon sources. Understanding this decoupling is critical for improving predictions of ecosystem carbon balance under increasing climate extremes, as highlighted by recent work on water scarcity and carbon sinking potential in California.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during compound drought-heat events, the sensitivity of Reco to soil temperature and antecedent moisture exceeds that of GPP to vapor pressure deficit and soil moisture, leading to a disproportionate increase in the Reco/GPP ratio and a shift in net ecosystem exchange.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing FLUXNET2015 and regional AmeriFlux datasets (pre-2026) to analyze eddy covariance time series from ecosystems with documented drought sensitivity (e.g., Mediterranean grasslands, oak savannas, restored wetlands). We will apply a conditional sampling algorithm to isolate compound drought-heat spells from the meteorological record. The decoupled response will be quantified using bivariate nonlinear response functions of GPP and Reco to soil moisture and temperature, and by analyzing the hysteresis in their diurnal and seasonal relationships. Structural equation modeling will partition the relative controls of soil moisture deficit, air temperature, and vapor pressure deficit on the GPP-Reco imbalance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, data-driven framework for how photosynthesis and respiration decouple under extreme conditions, moving beyond the assumption of coupled responses. It will directly inform the parameterization of land surface models by identifying critical thresholds where carbon balance predictions fail. The analysis will also extend the researcher's expertise in wetland and semi-arid ecosystem fluxes to a synthetic, cross-biome understanding of carbon-climate feedbacks.
proposals.novelty: While drought impacts on fluxes are well-studied, the explicit, quantitative testing of the coupled-response assumption during compound extremes using a multi-site network approach is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates the researcher's recent focus on methane hotspots and water scarcity by examining the fundamental carbon flux responses that underpin greenhouse gas budgets.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Pulsed Nature of Ecosystem Respiration: Linking Discrete Hydrological and Thermal Events to CO2 and CH4 Flux Hot Moments
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem respiration (Reco) is a dominant but poorly constrained flux in the global carbon cycle. Current biogeochemical models often treat Reco as a steady, temperature-driven background process, potentially misrepresenting its true temporal dynamics and magnitude. This is critical because episodic pulses or 'hot moments' of respiration, particularly those involving methane, can disproportionately influence annual greenhouse gas budgets, as seen in wetland and agricultural systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ecosystem respiration is fundamentally pulsed, with discrete, detectable hot moments triggered by specific environmental cues—primarily rapid shifts in water table depth and soil temperature—that are not captured by steady-state models. These pulses will be most pronounced in ecosystems with dynamic hydrology, such as restored wetlands and irrigated perennial crops.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term, high-frequency eddy covariance flux data (CO2, CH4, H2O, energy) and co-located sensor data (soil temperature, moisture, water table) from established networks (e.g., AmeriFlux, FLUXNET-CH4) across California ecosystems, including restored wetlands and alfalfa fields. We will apply a two-step analytical framework: 1) Use change-point detection algorithms (e.g., Pruned Exact Linear Time) on the Reco time series to statistically identify pulse onset and cessation. 2) Employ causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PCMCI) to rigorously test for causal links between identified respiration pulses and candidate environmental drivers (precipitation, irrigation, temperature spikes, drying/rewetting cycles).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, event-based paradigm for modeling ecosystem respiration, moving beyond mean-condition parameterizations. It will yield a quantitative framework to predict the timing and magnitude of respiration hot moments, directly improving the accuracy of bottom-up greenhouse gas budgets and informing management practices in climate-vulnerable ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While hot moments are acknowledged, this study innovates by applying causal inference and breakpoint detection directly to continuous flux data to systematically define, attribute, and model respiration pulses as discrete events, rather than as statistical noise around a steady process.

proposals.title: Legacies of Disturbance: Quantifying the Duration and Recovery Trajectories of Net Ecosystem Carbon Exchange Following Fire, Drought, and Land-Use Change
proposals.motivation: Disturbances like fire, drought, and land-use conversion are major, episodic drivers of ecosystem carbon loss, but their long-term legacy effects on carbon fluxes remain poorly quantified. Understanding the duration and recovery trajectory of net ecosystem carbon exchange (NEE) is critical for improving ecosystem models, informing climate mitigation strategies, and managing landscapes for carbon resilience, especially in disturbance-prone regions like California's ecosystems. This directly addresses a key uncertainty in the continental-scale greenhouse gas flux network's ability to account for non-steady-state conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the recovery of NEE to pre-disturbance levels follows a predictable, disturbance-specific trajectory, where recovery time is a function of disturbance intensity, post-disturbance water availability, and ecosystem type, with managed ecosystems showing slower or incomplete recovery.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term, pre-2026 eddy covariance data from FLUXNET and regional networks (e.g., AmeriFlux) to identify sites with documented disturbances. Using a state-of-the-art data-driven approach, we will apply a Bayesian change point detection algorithm (e.g., Bayesian Online Changepoint Detection) to objectively identify the timing of disturbance events from NEE time series. Subsequently, we will fit non-linear recovery models (e.g., sigmoidal or exponential asymptotic functions) to the post-disturbance NEE data, parameterizing recovery time constants and asymptotic recovery levels. Covariates including water level, soil temperature, and remotely-sensed vegetation indices will be integrated to explain variance in recovery trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-disturbance, data-driven quantification of carbon flux recovery timescales across a range of ecosystems. It will yield empirical recovery functions that can be directly incorporated into terrestrial biosphere models (e.g., CLM, ORCHIDEE) to improve their representation of disturbance legacies. The results will offer a critical benchmark for assessing the carbon sink potential of restored and recovering landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies document immediate carbon losses, this analysis systematically quantifies the *duration* and *shape* of the recovery trajectory across disturbance types using objective statistical detection and modeling, moving beyond single-site case studies to a synthetic network-level analysis.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Interactive Effects of Hydrological, Thermal, and Biotic Stresses on Interannual Variability of Net Ecosystem Carbon Uptake
proposals.motivation: Year-to-year decreases in net carbon uptake (NEE) threaten the stability of ecosystem carbon sinks, a critical buffer against climate change. While individual stresses like drought or heat are studied, they often co-occur in nature, and their interactive, non-linear effects on carbon fluxes are poorly quantified. This gap limits our ability to predict ecosystem responses to increasing climate variability, particularly in water-limited and managed ecosystems central to regional carbon budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interannual declines in net carbon uptake are not caused by single stresses but by specific, synergistic combinations of hydrological (soil moisture, water table), thermal (air/soil temperature, VPD), and biotic (phenology, disturbance) factors, whose relative importance varies by ecosystem type.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term eddy covariance data from FLUXNET (e.g., AmeriFlux, ICOS) for diverse ecosystems (wetlands, grasslands, croplands). Using a causal inference framework (e.g., Structural Equation Modeling - SEM) combined with multivariate extreme event detection, we will identify co-occurring stressor combinations preceding NEE declines. Machine learning (Random Forest with SHAP analysis) will then be used to model and attribute the marginal and interactive contributions of each stressor to the observed carbon flux anomalies, validated against chamber-based methane and respiration measurements where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a generalized framework for diagnosing compound stress events on carbon fluxes, moving beyond correlation to causal attribution. It will deliver ecosystem-specific 'stress fingerprints' that explain interannual carbon sink variability, directly informing land management and climate models about critical vulnerability thresholds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, multi-stressor attribution approach applied to flux tower data, explicitly testing for interactions rather than individual drivers, and integrating causal inference with machine learning to disentangle complex, real-world stressor combinations affecting ecosystem carbon exchange.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Data-Model Fusion Framework for Scaling Methane and Carbon Dioxide Fluxes from Tower to Landscape Using Harmonized Eddy Covariance and Sentinel-2/Sentinel-5P Data
proposals.motivation: Eddy covariance (EC) towers provide direct, high-frequency measurements of ecosystem carbon, water, and methane fluxes, but their spatial representativeness is limited. Satellite remote sensing offers broad spatial coverage but lacks direct flux measurements. A robust, scalable fusion method is critically needed to quantify greenhouse gas budgets across heterogeneous landscapes, particularly for ecosystems like managed wetlands and water-stressed agriculture where fluxes are highly sensitive to water level and vegetation dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid approach combining data-driven machine learning with process-informed constraints will significantly improve the accuracy and spatial extrapolation of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) fluxes when fusing EC data with multi-spectral and atmospheric composition satellite products.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage fusion framework. First, we will harmonize EC data from networks like AmeriFlux/FLUXNET with concurrent satellite data (Sentinel-2 for vegetation indices/water indices, Sentinel-5P for column CH4). Second, we will train a Random Forest model using EC fluxes as targets and satellite-derived predictors, alongside key EC-measured drivers (soil temperature, water table depth). Third, we will integrate the machine learning outputs as a prior into a simple process model (e.g., a light-use efficiency model for CO2, a temperature/water table model for CH4) using a Bayesian inversion to constrain regional estimates and generate uncertainty-quantified flux maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a reproducible, open-source fusion pipeline that translates point-scale EC observations into landscape-scale flux estimates with defined uncertainties. It will directly address scaling challenges highlighted in recent methane and carbon sink studies, providing a methodological advance for regional greenhouse gas budgeting and for validating emerging satellite-based flux products.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have used either machine learning or process models alone for scaling, our hybrid approach explicitly uses the data-driven model to inform and constrain a parsimonious process model, combining the strengths of both methods to improve physical interpretability and extrapolation capability beyond the training data range.

proposals.title: Quantifying and Partitioning Systematic Errors in Eddy Covariance Fluxes: A Multi-Site, Multi-Tracer Framework for Water, Carbon, and Methane
proposals.motivation: The eddy covariance (EC) method is foundational for quantifying ecosystem-atmosphere exchanges of water, carbon, and methane, yet persistent systematic errors from sensor separation, spectral attenuation, and non-ideal terrain compromise flux accuracy and comparability across networks. These errors propagate into uncertain assessments of ecosystem carbon sinks, water use efficiency, and methane budgets, which are critical for evaluating climate feedbacks and management outcomes, as highlighted in Baldocchi's recent work on wetlands, alfalfa, and flux networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, multi-tracer analysis can isolate and quantify distinct sources of systematic EC error (e.g., high-frequency loss vs. advective bias), and that these errors exhibit predictable, site-specific dependencies on environmental drivers like turbulence, water table depth, and canopy structure.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term data from contrasting FLUXNET sites (wetland, cropland, forest) where concurrent EC measurements of H2O, CO2, and CH4 exist. The approach will: 1) apply and compare established spectral correction methods (e.g., Moncrieff et al., 1997; Fratini et al., 2012) to quantify high-frequency loss; 2) use the CH4 and H2O fluxes, with their different source/sink distributions, as tracers to diagnose low-frequency biases and advective errors; and 3) employ a Bayesian framework to partition total uncertainty among these error sources, relating their magnitudes to concurrent measurements of atmospheric stability, friction velocity, and soil/water variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a transparent, generalized framework for diagnosing systematic EC errors, moving beyond generic uncertainty estimates. It will directly improve the interpretation of flux data for key greenhouse gases, enabling more robust comparisons of carbon and water fluxes across ecosystems and more accurate evaluation of management impacts on ecosystem-climate interactions.
proposals.novelty: While spectral corrections are common, the simultaneous use of CH4 and H2O as tracers to diagnose different error mechanisms within a unified Bayesian partitioning framework is novel. This approach directly addresses the complex error structure in multi-tracer studies of ecosystems with strong biophysical drivers, such as the wetlands and water-stressed agroecosystems central to Baldocchi's recent research.

proposals.title: Causal Discovery of Carbon Flux Drivers in Semi-Arid Ecosystems: Integrating Eddy Covariance, Soil, and Atmospheric Data
proposals.motivation: Understanding the true drivers of ecosystem carbon flux (NEE) is critical for predicting carbon sequestration under climate change, yet traditional correlation-based analyses from flux towers cannot disentangle causation from co-variation. This is particularly urgent in water-limited ecosystems like California's semi-arid regions, where complex interactions between water availability, temperature, and soil processes control carbon cycling, as highlighted in Baldocchi's recent work on alfalfa systems. Applying causal inference to long-term flux data can move the field beyond descriptive patterns to mechanistic, testable models of ecosystem function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that causal discovery algorithms will reveal that soil moisture is a direct causal driver of midday NEE in semi-arid ecosystems, while air temperature's influence is largely indirect, mediated through vapor pressure deficit (VPD) and its effect on stomatal conductance. Furthermore, we hypothesize that antecedent soil moisture conditions exert a causal influence on the sensitivity of NEE to current environmental drivers.
proposals.method: We will apply the Peter-Clark (PC) and Fast Causal Inference (FCI) algorithms to high-frequency, multi-year eddy covariance and ancillary data (soil moisture, temperature, PAR, VPD, precipitation) from established sites like Tonzi Ranch. These constraint-based causal discovery methods, implemented in the Tetrad or `pcalg` R package, can distinguish direct from indirect causes and suggest causal structures from observational data. We will structure the analysis using time-lagged variables to account for memory effects and validate the inferred Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) using independent data splits and known physical constraints from the literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a causal model of carbon flux for semi-arid ecosystems, providing a more robust framework for predicting carbon sink strength under drought. It will demonstrate the practical utility of causal discovery methods in environmental sciences, moving flux data analysis from correlation to causation. The resulting DAGs will identify key leverage points for management or future experimental manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference is emerging in climate science, its application to ecosystem flux tower data is novel, offering a principled way to integrate the complex, multivariate time series central to Baldocchi's research. This approach goes beyond the regression and model-data fusion techniques common pre-2026 to explicitly test causal hypotheses embedded in process-based models.

proposals.title: Constraining Ecosystem Carbon and Water Fluxes by Assimilating Eddy Covariance Data into a Process Model Using an Ensemble Kalman Filter
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem process models (e.g., BEPS, CLM) are essential for predicting carbon and water fluxes under climate change, but their parameters are often poorly constrained, leading to high uncertainty. Directly integrating high-frequency, multi-year eddy covariance flux data—a core strength of networks like AmeriFlux and FLUXNET—into these models remains a major challenge. This limits our ability to produce robust, site-specific forecasts of ecosystem responses to droughts and heatwaves, as highlighted in recent studies on water-scarce ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that assimilating observed net ecosystem exchange (NEE), latent heat (LE), and sensible heat (H) fluxes using an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) will significantly reduce uncertainty in key model parameters (e.g., Vcmax, stomatal conductance, soil respiration Q10), leading to improved predictions of ecosystem carbon and water dynamics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EnKF, a proven data assimilation technique from numerical weather prediction, to the ecosystem model BEPS. The EnKF will be implemented to sequentially assimilate half-hourly eddy covariance data from long-term sites (e.g., a restored wetland and a semi-arid alfalfa field). An ensemble of model states and parameters will be propagated forward, with observations used to update both states and a defined set of sensitive parameters. The technical workflow will involve coupling the EnKF algorithm with BEPS, defining observation and model error covariance matrices based on flux data quality assessments, and conducting twin experiments to validate the system before application to real data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operational framework for rigorously constraining ecosystem models with flux tower data, directly addressing the need for more predictive models. It will yield improved, data-informed estimates of carbon sequestration and water use efficiency for contrasting ecosystems. The methodology will provide a template for the broader flux community to reduce model uncertainty and better quantify ecosystem-climate feedbacks.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is common in meteorology, its application to fully integrate high-frequency flux data into process-based ecosystem models for joint carbon-water parameter optimization is not yet routine. This proposal uniquely combines Dr. Baldocchi's expertise in flux measurements and methane/water stress with advanced EnKF techniques to move beyond model-data intercomparison towards true integration.

proposals.title: Constraining Tower-Level Net Ecosystem Exchange with Regional Atmospheric Inversions: A Bottom-Up Validation and Correction Framework
proposals.motivation: Regional atmospheric inversions provide top-down constraints on carbon budgets, but their coarse resolution (50-100 km) limits their utility for validating individual eddy covariance tower flux measurements, which are critical for process understanding and model parameterization. This mismatch in scale creates uncertainty when reconciling bottom-up and top-down flux estimates, hindering our ability to accurately quantify ecosystem carbon sequestration, especially in heterogeneous landscapes like California's mosaic of agricultural, wetland, and semi-arid ecosystems. A method to downscale regional atmospheric constraints to the tower footprint would directly address this scale gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian data assimilation framework, which inversely applies regional atmospheric inversion constraints to a high-resolution land surface model, can identify and correct systematic biases in individual tower flux measurements (NEE) by optimizing the spatial distribution of fluxes within the inversion grid cell.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical Bayesian framework. First, we will use the Weather Research and Forecasting model coupled to chemistry (WRF-Chem) or a Lagrangian particle dispersion model (e.g., STILT) to generate high-resolution (<1 km) footprint sensitivity matrices linking surface fluxes from the tower's source area to the concentration measurements informing the regional inversion. We will then assimilate the regional inversion's net flux constraint for a grid cell, along with multiple tower NEE and ancillary data (soil moisture, temperature, NDVI), into the Community Land Model (CLM) at a fine scale. The inversion will optimize surface flux parameters within the CLM to satisfy both the tower-level processes and the regional atmospheric constraint, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to quantify uncertainties.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable methodology for objectively validating and bias-correcting tower flux data using independent atmospheric constraints. It will directly improve the accuracy of continental-scale flux networks (like AmeriFlux and ICOS) by providing a top-down quality control metric. The resulting corrected, spatially explicit fluxes will enhance our ability to attribute carbon source/sink dynamics to specific land cover types and management practices within heterogeneous regions.
proposals.novelty: While inversions are typically used to scale up, we propose a novel downscaling to directly correct individual tower data. The approach uniquely integrates high-resolution atmospheric transport modeling with ecosystem process modeling in an inverse framework specifically targeted at the tower footprint scale, moving beyond simple comparison to active constraint.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Annual Net Carbon Balance of Diffuse Light Enhancement and Respiration Pulses in a Water-Limited Ecosystem
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem carbon budgets are typically modeled using mean climatic drivers, but short-term, non-linear processes like diffuse light enhancement of photosynthesis and temperature-driven respiration pulses can significantly bias annual sums. In water-limited ecosystems like California's semi-arid grasslands or managed alfalfa, these transient events interact with soil moisture stress, making their net impact on annual Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) uncertain. Accurately scaling these processes is critical for robust carbon sink assessments and predicting ecosystem responses to climate extremes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a semi-arid ecosystem, the annual net carbon impact of diffuse light enhancement events is positive but substantially reduced by concurrent respiration pulses, and that the magnitude of this net effect is modulated by antecedent soil moisture conditions.
proposals.method: We will use 5+ years of eddy covariance flux, spectral radiation, and soil sensor data from a Tonzi Ranch-like oak savanna or a similar semi-arid site. Diffuse light events will be identified using the diffuse fraction (Df) from spectral radiometry, and respiration pulses from nighttime NEE partitioned using the Reichstein 2005 algorithm. We will employ a conditional sampling and gap-filling framework to isolate the NEE during these events. Annual budgets will be constructed by summing the isolated fluxes and comparing them to traditional, non-conditioned gap-filled annual sums to quantify the bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, process-attributed quantification of how two key transient phenomena collectively shape the annual carbon budget in a water-limited system. It will deliver a methodological framework for partitioning event-driven fluxes within long-term records. The results will directly inform model parameterization for better predicting ecosystem carbon cycling under increasing atmospheric aerosol loading and heatwave frequency.
proposals.novelty: While diffuse light and respiration pulses are studied individually, their competing, moisture-modulated effects on the *annual net carbon balance* are rarely quantified empirically. This study moves beyond documenting the processes to rigorously accounting for their integrated, and potentially offsetting, budgetary impacts.

proposals.title: Coupling Autotrophic Respiration to Photosynthetic Allocation: A Mechanistic Constraint for Ecosystem Flux Models
proposals.motivation: Current ecosystem models often treat respiration (R) and photosynthesis (GPP) as semi-independent processes, linked by empirical ratios or environmental scalars, limiting their predictive power under novel climate and disturbance regimes. This decoupling is a major source of uncertainty in predicting net ecosystem carbon exchange (NEE), especially in water-limited or managed ecosystems where carbon allocation shifts rapidly. A model where R is explicitly derived from recent photosynthetic products would provide a more physiologically grounded constraint, improving forecasts of ecosystem carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: Autotrophic respiration (Ra) can be robustly modeled as a direct function of the allocation and utilization of recent photosynthetic products, and this explicit coupling will reduce model uncertainty in predicting daily to seasonal NEE across diverse ecosystems compared to traditional temperature-driven respiration models.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a model framework where GPP, estimated via a light-use efficiency approach, is allocated to labile and structural pools with time-lagged turnover. Ra will be calculated as the sum of maintenance respiration (scaled from pool sizes and tissue nitrogen) and growth respiration (a fixed fraction of carbon allocated to new growth). The model will be parameterized and validated using multi-year eddy covariance flux data (CO2, H2O, CH4) and ancillary measurements from the AmeriFlux/FLUXNET network, focusing on sites with contrasting water regimes (e.g., restored wetland, semi-arid grassland, alfalfa field). Bayesian calibration and comparison to a standard Q10-based respiration model will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a tractable, process-based model that explicitly links Ra to GPP, reducing the number of free parameters related to respiration. It will directly improve the mechanistic interpretation of eddy covariance data, particularly for disentangling autotrophic and heterotrophic respiration contributions. The model will be a valuable tool for assessing carbon sink vulnerability in ecosystems facing water scarcity or restoration.
proposals.novelty: While allocation schemes exist, the novelty lies in rigorously formulating and testing respiration as a *direct mechanistic consequence* of photosynthetic allocation in an operational ecosystem flux model, moving beyond correlation to causation. This approach integrates Baldocchi's expertise in water-carbon-flux interactions with emerging pre-2026 needs to constrain respiration processes.

proposals.title: Downscaling CMIP6 Climate Projections to Quantify Microclimate-Driven Uncertainty in Regional Carbon and Methane Flux Upscaling
proposals.motivation: Upscaling eddy covariance flux tower measurements to regions is critical for assessing ecosystem carbon and greenhouse gas budgets, but is limited by coarse-resolution climate model data that poorly represent the heterogeneous environmental drivers (e.g., temperature, humidity, soil moisture) controlling fluxes. This mismatch is acute in complex terrain and heterogeneous ecosystems like wetlands and managed agricultural systems, where microclimates strongly modulate methane emissions and water-carbon trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using statistically downscaled, high-resolution (1-km) climate data from CMIP6 models will significantly reduce the uncertainty in spatially extrapolated carbon and methane fluxes by better capturing the local environmental gradients that drive ecosystem processes.
proposals.method: We will apply the Multivariate Adaptive Constructed Analogs (MACA v2) statistical downscaling method to bias-corrected CMIP6 outputs (e.g., from GFDL-ESM4, UKESM1-0-LL) over California, generating 1-km daily fields of air temperature, humidity, and precipitation for historical and future scenarios. These downscaled drivers will be integrated into a machine learning-based flux upscaling framework, using Random Forest and Gradient Boosting models trained on FLUXNET2015 and AmeriFlux data (including CH4 where available). The spatial flux predictions from coarse vs. downscaled drivers will be compared across key ecoregions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available high-resolution climate-forced flux dataset for California, directly quantifying the uncertainty in regional budgets introduced by climate driver resolution. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating next-generation climate projections into terrestrial biogeochemical upscaling, improving assessments of climate change impacts on carbon sinks and methane hotspots.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling is common in climate impacts research, its explicit application to improve the environmental drivers for ecosystem flux upscaling—particularly for methane—and to quantify the resulting reduction in budget uncertainty is a novel synthesis at the intersection of climatology and biogeochemistry.

proposals.title: A Pan-Biome Validation of the Hybrid Ball-Berry Model for Predicting Ecosystem-Scale Water and Carbon Fluxes
proposals.motivation: The hybrid Ball-Berry model, which integrates empirical and optimization approaches for stomatal conductance, shows promise for improving land surface model predictions of water and carbon fluxes. However, its validation has been limited to a few ecosystem types, primarily forests and croplands. A comprehensive test across a wider range of biomes—including wetlands, shrublands, and water-stressed ecosystems—is critical to assess its robustness and generalizability for global flux upscaling and climate projections.
proposals.hypothesis: The hybrid Ball-Berry model will accurately predict ecosystem-scale evapotranspiration and gross primary productivity across diverse biomes, but its performance will be systematically influenced by biome-specific plant functional traits and environmental stressors, particularly water scarcity.
proposals.method: We will leverage data from the FLUXNET2015 dataset and ancillary AmeriFlux sites, selecting 30+ tower sites representing major biomes (e.g., wetland, grassland, savanna, shrubland, evergreen/deciduous forest, cropland). The hybrid model, combining the empirical Ball-Berry slope with a water-use efficiency optimization term, will be implemented and parameterized using site-level meteorological drivers (temperature, VPD, radiation). Model predictions of latent heat and CO2 fluxes will be validated against half-hourly eddy covariance measurements. We will employ Bayesian model-data fusion to constrain key parameters (m, g1) and analyze residuals in relation to soil moisture, leaf area index, and plant functional type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, biome-specific evaluation of a key stomatal conductance model, directly informing its utility in next-generation land models. It will quantify model biases under water stress and in non-forested ecosystems, leading to improved parameterizations for global flux networks and reducing uncertainty in continental-scale carbon and water cycle estimates.
proposals.novelty: While the hybrid Ball-Berry concept exists, a systematic cross-biome validation at the ecosystem scale, explicitly linking model performance to water scarcity and disturbance regimes (informed by Baldocchi's recent work on wetlands and alfalfa), has not been conducted. This moves beyond single-biome studies to test fundamental model assumptions about stomatal behavior across the plant functional spectrum.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Ecosystem Respiration Parameters from Eddy Covariance Fluxes Using Automatic Differentiation
proposals.motivation: Ecosystem respiration (Reco) is a critical but poorly constrained component of the terrestrial carbon cycle. Its temperature sensitivity (Q10) and basal rate (Rref) are notoriously difficult to measure directly at ecosystem scales, leading to significant uncertainty in carbon budget projections. This proposal addresses the inverse problem of estimating these key parameters from widely available, long-term eddy covariance flux data, leveraging computational advances to improve process understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a process-based model, coupled with automatic differentiation for gradient-based optimization, can robustly and simultaneously estimate the temperature sensitivity (Q10) and basal respiration rate (Rref) of ecosystem respiration from net ecosystem exchange (NEE) data, and that these parameters will reveal systematic differences in carbon cycling between water-stressed and well-watered ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will implement a modified Lloyd & Taylor respiration model within a differentiable programming framework (e.g., JAX or PyTorch). Using multi-year eddy covariance data from contrasting sites (e.g., restored wetland and semi-arid alfalfa from Baldocchi's recent work), we will formulate a Bayesian inverse problem. The model will ingest partitioned nighttime NEE (as Reco) and soil temperature. We will use gradient descent algorithms (e.g., Adam) to minimize the mismatch between modeled and observed fluxes, directly computing gradients of the loss function with respect to Q10 and Rref via automatic differentiation, enabling efficient and precise parameter estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally efficient method to derive hard-to-measure physiological parameters from standard flux tower data. It will yield spatially and temporally explicit estimates of respiration parameters, directly linking them to environmental drivers like soil moisture and temperature, thereby improving the parameterization of land surface models.
proposals.novelty: While inverse modeling is established, the application of automatic differentiation to this specific biogeochemical problem is novel. It moves beyond traditional Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods by enabling faster, more scalable optimization and direct sensitivity analysis, offering a new pathway to constrain ecosystem models with flux data.

proposals.title: Resolving Canopy Turbulence and Methane Hot Spots: A High-Resolution CFD Study of Wind-Soil-Vegetation Interactions in a Restored Wetland
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of ecosystem-scale methane (CH4) fluxes is critical for climate modeling, but significant uncertainty stems from sub-canopy turbulence and its role in creating and transporting 'hot spots'. Traditional eddy covariance methods struggle to resolve the complex, three-dimensional flow within vegetation, particularly in heterogeneous ecosystems like restored wetlands where water level and soil disturbance create fine-scale source patterns. Applying computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to this problem can bridge the gap between plot-level biogeochemistry and tower-based flux measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-resolution CFD simulations will reveal that persistent, coherent sub-canopy flow structures, modulated by canopy architecture and water table depth, are the primary physical mechanism controlling the spatial integration and magnitude of methane fluxes measured at the ecosystem scale.
proposals.method: We will employ Large-Eddy Simulation (LES) using the PALM model (version 6.0) to simulate turbulent flow within a virtual domain replicating the Tonzi Ranch restored wetland site. The simulation will incorporate a realistic plant area density profile derived from terrestrial lidar scans and a dynamic lower boundary condition representing soil methane source strength, parameterized from chamber measurements of water level and soil disturbance. Scalar transport of a passive CH4 tracer will be simulated to link subsurface sources to above-canfluxes. Model validation will use concurrent 3D sonic anemometer data and eddy covariance fluxes from the site.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, three-dimensional framework to explain how subsurface biogeochemical hot spots translate into ecosystem-scale fluxes. It will directly improve the interpretation of flux tower data in complex terrain and inform better parameterizations for land-surface models. The coupled CFD-biogeochemistry approach will advance the physical understanding of trace gas exchange in non-ideal, heterogeneous ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While CFD-LES has been applied to idealized canopies, its integration with empirical, spatially explicit methane source maps from restored wetlands—directly linking soil disturbance states to atmospheric transport—is novel. This moves beyond simulating neutral scalars like heat to actively coupling the model with the ecosystem-specific methane production dynamics central to Dr. Baldocchi's recent research.

proposals.title: Downscaling Regional Atmospheric Observations to Canopy Environmental Drivers: A Data-Model Fusion Approach for Validating Ecosystem Fluxes
proposals.motivation: Regional-scale satellite and atmospheric data are essential for upscaling ecosystem carbon and water fluxes, but they often fail to capture the sub-grid, canopy-level environmental conditions (e.g., photosynthetically active radiation (PAR), air temperature) that directly drive these processes. This mismatch introduces significant uncertainty when validating or parameterizing land surface models, particularly in heterogeneous landscapes like the restored wetlands and managed agroecosystems central to Baldocchi's recent work on methane and carbon sinks. A robust downscaling method is needed to bridge the scale gap between regional atmospheric observations and the canopy environment where biophysical processes occur.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on co-located eddy covariance tower measurements and regional atmospheric reanalysis data can accurately reconstruct high-frequency, canopy-level environmental drivers, and that these downscaled drivers will significantly improve the explanatory power of models for ecosystem methane and carbon dioxide fluxes.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term data from the AmeriFlux/FLUXNET network, including sites in California wetlands and alfalfa fields. For each site, we will pair high-temporal-resolution tower measurements of canopy PAR, air temperature, and soil moisture with corresponding coarse-scale variables from the ERA5 reanalysis (e.g., surface solar radiation, 2m air temperature). A Random Forest or Gradient Boosting model will be trained to predict the canopy conditions from the ERA5 drivers, explicitly incorporating site-specific modifiers like topographic wetness index and normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) from Landsat. The trained model will then be applied regionally to generate 30m resolution maps of canopy drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable framework for deriving canopy-relevant environmental data from regional atmospheric products, directly addressing a key scale mismatch in land-atmosphere interaction studies. It will enhance the accuracy of bottom-up flux estimates and model validation, particularly for trace gases like methane where production is highly sensitive to microclimate. The resulting data product will be a critical tool for diagnosing hotspots of flux activity identified in prior wetland and agricultural studies.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling climate data is common, this approach uniquely fuses atmospheric reanalysis with *in-situ* flux tower data to target the specific canopy-level drivers of biogeochemical processes, moving beyond simple spatial interpolation to a physically informed, ecosystem-specific method. It directly connects Baldocchi's expertise in trace gas flux networks with emerging needs in spatial scaling.

ideas.title: Apply a Model-Data Fusion Framework from Meteorology to Validate mWUE-VPD Functions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Numerical Weather Prediction to validate mWUE-VPD functions across underrepresented biomes. In meteorology, data assimilation frameworks like the Ensemble Kalman Filter are used to fuse imperfect dynamical models with heterogeneous observational data, identifying and correcting model biases across diverse conditions. Applying this analogy, we propose treating the Michaelis-Menten or quadratic mWUE-VPD functions as testable biophysical models. A systematic model-data fusion campaign should be undertaken, using global eddy covariance data (e.g., FLUXNET) as the observational constraint. By assimilating data from underrepresented vegetation types and climates, an inverse modeling approach can quantify functional biases, iteratively adjust parameters, and objectively determine the most robust functional form for different biome-climate clusters, thereby rigorously validating and improving the proposed relationships where they are currently most uncertain.

ideas.title: Automated synthesis of emergent ecosystem properties from standardized ancillary data
ideas.description: This project develops a new synthesis engine that is explicitly designed to exploit the expanded variable set from an enhanced BASE pipeline. By standardizing a comprehensive suite of ancillary measurements (e.g., soil biogeochemistry, canopy traits, spectral data) using the same rigorous framework as core fluxes, it creates a multi-dimensional, feature-rich data product. The automated synthesis tools are then built with machine learning and causal inference methods to mine this product, not for pre-defined analyses, but to autonomously identify novel, robust correlations and emergent ecosystem properties across global site networks. The core synergy is that the expansion of variables dictates the design of the synthesis tools, transforming them from general-purpose aggregators into automated hypothesis generators for complex ecosystem functioning.

ideas.title: Applying Fourier and Wavelet Analysis from Signal Processing to Decompose mWUE-VPD Dynamics Across Temporal Scales
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of scaling the sub-daily mWUE-VPD relationship by drawing an analogy from signal processing. In that field, complex signals are decomposed using Fourier and Wavelet transforms to understand multi-scale dynamics. The adapted research idea is to treat the mWUE and VPD time series as coupled signals. By applying these decomposition techniques, the research would systematically isolate and examine the diurnal, seasonal, and interannual components of their relationship. This approach can reveal if the sub-daily mechanistic link persists as a dominant mode at longer scales, or if it is overridden by other processes (e.g., phenology, soil moisture depletion), providing a quantitative framework for scale transition in plant water-use efficiency.

ideas.title: Personalized Model-Data Fusion for Stomatal Optimization
ideas.description: This project develops a lightweight, personal data pipeline specifically designed to enable individual researchers or small teams to rigorously test new stomatal optimization theories within terrestrial biosphere models. By integrating tools to ingest, quality-control, and format single-site eddy covariance and meteorological data, the pipeline allows researchers to directly implement the observed non-linear mWUE-VPD relationship into a model's stomatal scheme and evaluate its performance against their local observations. This creates a powerful, accessible platform for hypothesis-driven model development, moving beyond large-model intercomparisons to personalized, iterative model-data fusion. The synergy enables rapid, bottom-up improvement of core model processes by empowering domain experts to seamlessly bridge their field data with model code.

ideas.title: Causal discovery for ecosystem function using raw, heterogeneous flux networks
ideas.description: This research develops a novel synthesis framework that applies causal discovery methods directly to raw, heterogeneous flux tower data from networks like FLUXNET without requiring prior, lossy standardization. By treating site-specific metadata, instrumentation, and processing as part of the causal graph, the method aims to disentangle the direct effect of variables like Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD) on ecosystem water-use efficiency from confounding soil and site effects. This approach simultaneously validates causal structures across diverse sites and identifies which forms of data heterogeneity are ignorable versus which must be accounted for, creating a new pathway for rapid, large-scale ecological synthesis from existing observational data.

ideas.title: A Causal Mediation Framework with Instrumental Variables to Disentangle Direct VPD and Soil-Mediated Effects on mWUE
ideas.description: This research develops a robust causal inference framework that moves beyond standard discovery methods to explicitly separate the direct effect of vapor pressure deficit (VPD) on ecosystem water use efficiency (mWUE) from the effect mediated through soil moisture. The system integrates instrumental variable techniques, using remote sensing and reanalysis data, with structural causal modeling to account for unmeasured confounding and bidirectional feedbacks. It rigorously tests the proposed causal graph and estimated direct effects against quasi-experimental variation found at sites with sharp edaphic gradients or documented disturbance histories. This adversarially designed approach yields more credible, quantitative estimates of the direct atmospheric stress on ecosystem function, independent of soil moisture dynamics.

ideas.title: Git for Ecosystem Function: Version-Controlled Analysis of Multi-Scale mWUE Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction applies data versioning principles to the study of ecosystem water-use efficiency (mWUE) across seasonal and interannual scales. It establishes a Git-like repository for all data (e.g., flux tower, remote sensing, climate) and analysis pipelines used to derive mWUE-VPD relationships, creating a complete, auditable history of changes. This allows researchers to systematically test how scaling assumptions, data processing decisions, or inclusion of new data years alter the perceived long-term mWUE trends or resilience. The synergy enables a new paradigm of collaborative, reproducible science where the evolution of scientific understanding about ecosystem function is as traceable and debuggable as a software codebase.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Diagnostic Reference Ranges for Ecosystem-Specific QA/QC Flags
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from clinical laboratory medicine, where diagnostic quality control relies on population-specific reference ranges, to the challenge of ecosystem-specific QA/QC. In clinical settings, biomarker thresholds are stratified by patient demographics to distinguish true anomalies from normal variation. Similarly, for environmental sensors in different biomes, we propose to create ecosystem-specific quality control routines. These routines would be built on pre-defined 'ecosystem-reference ranges' that capture the unique statistical distributions, diurnal cycles, and parameter covariances (e.g., between CO2 flux and PAR, or between spectral indices) characteristic of specific ecosystems like boreal forests or coastal wetlands. This approach will allow for more precise flagging of measurement artifacts, sensor failures, or anomalous ecological events by identifying deviations from the expected biome-specific norm, thereby addressing the unique measurement challenges posed by different environmental contexts.

ideas.title: Test biome-specific mWUE-VPD function validity as a diagnostic for non-linear m* behavior under extreme aridity
ideas.description: This research direction systematically tests proposed mWUE-VPD functions (e.g., Michaelis-Menten, quadratic) across a global gradient of underrepresented, arid biomes. The core innovation is to treat significant deviations from these functions not merely as model failures, but as empirical evidence for the breakdown of the linear IWUE-mWUE relationship. By analyzing the environmental and physiological conditions coinciding with these deviations, the study aims to identify the specific aridity thresholds and biome traits that trigger a non-linear increase in m*, thereby unifying model validation with the discovery of ecosystem-level hydraulic limits.

ideas.title: A Universal QC Framework with Biome-Specific Parameterization for Ecosystem Flux Data
ideas.description: This project develops a hierarchical quality control system where a core, invariant set of QC flags is defined for eddy covariance and related data based on universal principles of instrument performance and atmospheric physics. To address ecosystem-specific challenges, the system employs configurable, transparent parameter sets and logic modules that map local phenomena onto the core flags. This ensures that data from all biomes share a common quality language, enabling seamless integration for continental and global synthesis while still capturing the nuanced integrity issues unique to each site. The output is a unified, scalable QC toolkit that prioritizes interoperability without sacrificing local diagnostic precision.

ideas.description: This project integrates the development of ecosystem-specific QA/QC routines with the validation of IWUE-VPD functional forms across diverse biomes. The core innovation is to use the biome-specific IWUE-VPD relationships, once validated, as a dynamic physiological benchmark within the QA/QC framework. This allows the system to flag data points not only for technical faults but also for ecophysiological anomalies that deviate from the expected functional response to vapor pressure deficit. The outcome is a unified, intelligent processing pipeline that simultaneously improves data quality and generates a novel, directly usable product—a validated, biome-specific stress detection flag—for flux networks and ecological forecasts.

ideas.title: A CI/CD Framework for Continuous Learning and Validation of Ecosystem mWUE Models
ideas.description: This project develops an automated pipeline that integrates machine learning for mWUE prediction with continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) principles. Every new flux tower data submission is automatically validated for quality and format, then used to retrain and test the random forest or neural network models in a controlled staging environment. This creates a living, adaptive modeling system where predictive performance is continuously monitored, and model updates are deployed only after passing automated benchmarks against a trusted core dataset. The synergy enables scalable, reproducible, and transparent ecological forecasting by institutionalizing model evolution alongside data curation.

ideas.title: Applying Material Phase Transition Models to Predict Non-Linear m* Behavior Under Extreme Aridity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a linear relationship between Intrinsic Water Use Efficiency (IWUE) and marginal Water Use Efficiency (mWUE, represented by m*) across all aridity levels by proposing it breaks down under extreme conditions. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where the linear stress-strain relationship (Hooke's Law) fails at high stress, leading to non-linear plastic deformation modeled via phase transition theory. The adapted research plan is to conceptualize extreme aridity as a critical ecological stress threshold. The investigation would first compile global flux-tower and ecophysiological data to empirically identify the aridity index at which the linear relationship diverges. Subsequently, it would develop and test a non-linear, potentially sigmoidal or piecewise, model for m* that incorporates mechanisms like hydraulic failure or carbon starvation thresholds, analogous to material yield points. This framework shifts the paradigm from a universal linear scaling to a context-dependent model with critical transitions.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Risk Factor Analysis to Decode Physiological Drivers of Site-Specific m*
ideas.description: This idea proposes to investigate the physiological drivers of site-specific m* (the slope linking Intrinsic and Marginal Water Use Efficiency) by drawing an analogy from epidemiology. In public health, the geographic variability in disease rates is systematically dissected using risk factor analysis to attribute proportions of the variance to specific, measurable causes. Similarly, we adapt this solution to decompose the variability in the IWUE-mWUE slope across ecosystems. The research will treat m* as an integrative physiological 'outcome' and apply multivariate statistical frameworks (like variance partitioning or structural equation modeling) to quantify the unique and interactive contributions of key plant hydraulic traits (e.g., xylem vulnerability, root distribution) and soil properties (e.g., porosity, depth). This approach will move beyond linking m* to co-varying site conditions and instead identify the primary physiological 'risk factors' that mechanistically drive its spatial pattern, offering a predictive framework for understanding ecosystem responses to drought.

ideas.title: Inverse Data Assimilation via Geophysical Retrospective Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Numerical Weather Prediction and Climate Reanalysis to the challenge of model-data fusion. In fields like climatology, reanalysis systems solve the inverse problem of constraining a fixed physical model with heterogeneous historical observations to produce a consistent retrospective dataset. Here, we invert that pipeline: the system ingests outputs from diverse, potentially black-box models as its input flow. The standardized BASE product serves as the authoritative, observational constraint. The core mechanism is a retrospective analysis engine that applies adaptive correction functions—inspired by data assimilation's incremental analysis updates or bias-correction algorithms—to the stream of model outputs. This process minimizes the discrepancy against the BASE standard across scales, simultaneously producing a fused, corrected data product and diagnostic metrics (e.g., bias maps, uncertainty estimates) for each source model. The solution addresses the SCALING_DUAL dimension by enabling the systematic integration and scaling of multiple model outputs against a unified benchmark.

ideas.title: Adapting a Pharmacological Interaction Model to Disentangle Soil Moisture and VPD Effects on mWUE
ideas.description: The core challenge of separating the effects of co-varying stressors (soil moisture and VPD) on marginal water use efficiency (mWUE) is analogous to the problem of disentangling drug interactions in pharmacology. Pharmacology solves this using factorial designs and response surface methodology (RSM) to map and model combined effects. Adapting this solution, the research would design a study (using controlled experiments, manipulative field studies, or stratified analysis of flux tower data) where levels of soil water deficit and VPD are treated as independent factors in a matrix. By applying RSM to the mWUE data, the analysis can produce a quantitative model that isolates the main effect of soil moisture, the main effect of VPD, and their interaction effect under extreme conditions, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic attribution of drivers.

ideas.title: Common Pool Infrastructure for Underrepresented Biome (CUBE) Initiative
ideas.description: This initiative establishes a consortium-funded, shared physical infrastructure platform to overcome the prohibitive startup costs of data collection in rare biomes. It deploys and maintains standardized, core sensor arrays for essential abiotic data (e.g., microclimate, soil moisture) across a network of logistically challenging sites, creating a guaranteed baseline data stream. This common-pool resource de-risks and incentivizes further research by providing a reliable foundation upon which individual researchers and institutions can add their own specialized instrumentation and experimental setups, effectively solving the initial scarcity problem to catalyze diverse, higher-level science.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Tiered Ingestion for High-Frequency Eddy Covariance Data
ideas.description: This idea expands the original scaling challenge by drawing an analogy from radio astronomy, where instruments like the SKA manage petabytes per day of high-frequency raw data. The analogous solution uses a multi-tiered, adaptive pipeline to separate real-time processing from raw data storage. Adapted to eddy covariance, the research idea proposes a new architecture with a Tier 1 'fast path' for near-real-time calculation of fluxes and statistics from the raw high-frequency time series at the edge, and a Tier 2 'fidelity path' that uses intelligent, event-based triggers to capture and store short segments of the complete raw data. This approach scales the system to handle higher frequencies by transforming a continuous storage problem into a managed, event-driven one, balancing computational load, storage costs, and scientific utility.

ideas.title: Real-time, High-Frequency Anomaly Detection for Eddy Covariance Network Integrity
ideas.description: This research develops a scalable data pipeline that ingests and processes raw, high-frequency (e.g., 10-20 Hz) eddy covariance time series in real-time. By applying adapted unsupervised anomaly detection models from cybersecurity directly to this high-volume raw data stream, the system can identify novel failure modes, sensor drifts, and non-stationary atmospheric events as they occur. This approach reveals anomalies in the fundamental turbulent structures and high-order moments that are lost during standard averaging, providing a more sensitive and immediate assessment of instrument health and data integrity across a distributed sensor network.

ideas.title: Apply Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to predict mWUE dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from fluid dynamics, where Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) successfully model non-linear systems by embedding physical laws into the learning process. The analogous problem of modeling turbulent flows with partial data and strong non-linearities is solved by training neural networks to minimize both data error and physics constraint error. Adapted to eco-physiology, we propose using a PINN to model the complex, non-linear mWUE-VPD relationship from flux data. The neural network will be trained to predict mWUE while being regularized by a loss term that penalizes outputs violating core principles of stomatal conductance and photosynthesis models, thereby producing more robust and theoretically consistent predictions of plant water-use efficiency dynamics.

ideas.title: Dual-Threshold Stomatal Regulation: Minimizing Hydraulic Risk Subject to Dynamic Carbon Bankruptcy Avoidance
ideas.description: This model reframes stomatal optimization as minimizing the risk of hydraulic failure (e.g., xylem embolism) while explicitly avoiding carbon starvation, treating the latter as a dynamic constraint rather than a competing objective. The core innovation is a 'carbon bankruptcy threshold'—a non-linear penalty function that becomes severe as internal carbon reserves approach a critical low level determined by maintenance respiration and repair costs. This creates a system where stomata open sufficiently to prevent carbon bankruptcy, but do so in the way that incurs the least additional hydraulic risk, effectively finding the safest path to meet essential carbon needs. The model is parameterized using plant trait data (e.g., non-structural carbohydrate concentrations, xylem vulnerability curves) and validated against diurnal and seasonal gas exchange measurements under drought.

ideas.title: Personal Plant Physio-Finance Lab: A lightweight pipeline for individual researchers to test stomatal optimization as portfolio theory
ideas.description: This project develops a lightweight, personal data management pipeline specifically designed for plant physiology researchers. It integrates tools to frame and analyze stomatal conductance data through an econophysics lens, treating carbon gain and water loss as a dynamic portfolio optimization problem. The system allows a single researcher to clean, model, and share their experimental or field data using this novel theoretical framework without requiring large-scale computational infrastructure. This democratizes the testing of cross-disciplinary theories, enabling rapid, personalized iteration between physiological measurement and financial risk-model validation.

ideas.title: Continuous Integration for High-Frequency Data Assimilation (CI-HFDA)
ideas.description: This research direction develops a CI/CD system specifically engineered for high-frequency eddy covariance data pipelines. Instead of just testing static data formats, the system continuously ingests synthetic and real high-frequency data streams to stress-test the scaling architecture's ingestion, processing, and storage layers. This enables automated detection of performance degradation, data loss, or processing bottlenecks that only emerge under true high-frequency loads. The synergy creates a feedback loop where the pipeline's scaling logic is itself continuously validated and improved, ensuring robustness as data frequency and volume increase.

ideas.title: A Modular, Ontology-Driven Framework for Standardizing Ancillary Ecosystem Variables
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the BASE pipeline's standardization capabilities to a broader range of ancillary and ecosystem measurement variables by drawing an analogy from biomedical informatics. The analogous solution is the OMOP Common Data Model, which uses a standardized relational schema and ontological vocabularies to harmonize disparate clinical data sources. Adapted to ecosystem science, the research would involve designing an 'Eco-CDM'—a modular, ontology-driven framework. This framework would define core data structures and controlled vocabularies for variables like soil metrics, spectral indices, and phenological stages, enabling a systematic Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) process. This would allow the BASE pipeline to integrate diverse, non-flux data types seamlessly, facilitating large-scale, synthetic analyses across heterogeneous datasets while preserving critical methodological context.

ideas.title: A Bidirectional, Self-Improving Data Ecosystem for Distributed Science
ideas.description: This research proposes a bidirectional data ecosystem for distributed scientific observation networks (e.g., sensor arrays, telescopes, field stations). A central authority dynamically generates and pushes not just static corrections, but executable, context-aware processing routines and calibration models to local sites. These sites then run these adaptive routines on raw data, which can themselves be tuned to local conditions or novel instrumental signatures. The processed results and metadata about the efficacy of the routines are then fed back to the center, creating a closed loop where the central 'understanding'—the models and processing logic—continuously learns and improves from distributed application, fundamentally accelerating the refinement of both data products and scientific methodology.

ideas.title: Anomaly-Aware Risk Minimization for Stomatal Regulation Models
ideas.description: This research fuses the conceptual shift of modeling stomatal regulation as water loss risk minimization with unsupervised anomaly detection techniques from cybersecurity. The core objective is to develop a dual-purpose framework where unsupervised ML algorithms continuously analyze flux tower and sensor network data not only to flag instrument failures but, critically, to identify subtle, systemic patterns indicative of actual plant physiological risk states. This allows for the iterative refinement of the risk-minimization model by filtering out technical noise and confirming its predictions against verified ecological signals. Consequently, the approach enables more reliable, network-scale testing of alternative stomatal optimization theories using inherently messy observational data.

ideas.title: Incorporate hydraulic state dynamics into stomatal optimization using a control theory framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the steady-state assumption in stomatal optimization models (like mWUE) by drawing an analogy from control theory in electrical engineering. There, dynamic state-space models and feedback controllers (e.g., model-predictive control) optimize system performance over time by continuously adjusting inputs based on the evolving state, relaxing the need for a steady-state assumption. Adapted to plant physiology, the research would incorporate dynamic plant hydraulic status (e.g., stem and leaf water potentials, hydraulic capacitance) and non-steady-state water fluxes as state variables. Stomatal conductance becomes the control variable optimized not for an instantaneous steady state, but over a forward time horizon, balancing predicted carbon gain against the risks of dynamic hydraulic failure and the costs of recharging storage. This creates a time-explicit optimization framework that can predict stomatal responses to fluctuating environmental drivers like light and vapor pressure deficit more realistically.

ideas.title: Federated Data Reconciliation for a Global Flux Network
ideas.description: This research designs a federated system where a global network of continental flux networks (e.g., BASE, ICOS, OzFlux) interoperates not just by sharing data, but by sharing and applying standardized data processing corrections. A central coordination layer, governed by community consensus, develops and pushes harmonized correction protocols and algorithm updates back to the source data repositories of each participating network. This inverts and closes the data flow loop, ensuring that improvements in data quality, gap-filling, or partitioning methods are consistently applied at the origin, making the entire federated system's historical record more coherent and traceable over time. The result is a dynamically improving, decentralized knowledge graph of ecosystem fluxes where both raw and value-added data products remain synchronized and comparable across the globe.
